text.title: ES Fundamentals Failure Modes
text.summary: Three foundational antipatterns when adopting ES. Symptom + root cause + fix per item.

antipattern.symptom: Developer "fixes a typo" in a stored event row directly in the DB. Downstream projections rebuilt from the log produce different state than the live projection that processed the pre-fix event.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treated events as editable data rather than append-only history.
antipattern.fix: Revert the edit. Append a corrective event (`OrderPriceCorrected`) that downstream projections can apply. Restore from backup if the stream is already corrupted.

antipattern.symptom: Aggregate state stored as a DB row alongside the event log. App reads state from the row, ignoring the log. Bugs in apply handlers go undetected because state was never actually computed from events.

root-cause.source: ES fundamentals — events as source of truth

antipattern.root-cause: Author added a "fast path" + forgot the log is the contract.
antipattern.fix: Remove the state table. Use the log as source of truth. Add snapshots (per `[[event-sourcing-snapshots]]`) as a perf cache that is provably rebuildable.

antipattern.symptom: Event log contains `OrderUpdated_v1`, `OrderUpdated_v2`, `OrderUpdated_v3`. Each event is a flat snapshot; downstream projections cannot tell what changed.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treated event sourcing as a state log; missed the business-intent dimension.
antipattern.fix: Refactor: replace generic Updated events with named business facts (`OrderShipped`, `OrderAddressChanged`, `OrderDiscountApplied`). For existing data, write upcasters that translate old events into new business-fact events on read.
